title: Axolotl
lesson:
Axolotl is a free, open-source tool that fine-tunes language models from a single configuration file. You do not write training code. You fill in a .yaml file — a plain text file of setting: value pairs — that says which model to start from, which dataset to feed it, and which fine-tuning method to use. Then you run one command and Axolotl does the rest. It wraps the messy machinery of Hugging Face, PEFT, and the TRL Library into one tidy front door.

Why a tool like this exists

Fine-tuning by hand means gluing together a dozen libraries: load the model, quantize it, attach a LoRA adapter, tokenize and format the data, set up the trainer, pick the learning rate, save checkpoints, and so on. Every step has knobs, and a wrong type or a missing argument breaks the whole run an hour in. Axolotl collects all those knobs into one file and ships sensible defaults, so a beginner can train a real model on day one instead of week three.

Analogy — the bread machine. Baking bread from scratch means measuring flour, proving yeast, kneading, timing the oven. A bread machine asks you to pour in the ingredients and pick a program; it handles the kneading and baking. Axolotl is the bread machine of fine-tuning. The YAML file is your ingredient list and program choice. You still need good ingredients (a clean dataset) and the right program (the matching method), but you are spared the manual kneading.

Second analogy — a recipe card versus loose notes. Writing training code by hand is like cooking from scribbled notes on twelve sticky pads — easy to lose one, easy to copy a number wrong. A YAML config is a single printed recipe card. Anyone can read it, copy it, tweak one line, and reproduce your exact dish. That reproducibility is the quiet superpower: you can share the card and a teammate gets the same loaf, instead of a slightly different one because they guessed a temperature. In real teams this matters enormously — a fine-tune you cannot reproduce six months later is a fine-tune you cannot safely improve or debug.

What the config controls

A typical Axolotl config covers a few groups of settings: the base model to start from, the dataset and its format, the method (full fine-tune, LoRA, or QLoRA), and the training hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, how many passes over the data). Here is a trimmed example for a small QLoRA run:

base_model: meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B
load_in_4bit: true          # QLoRA: load base in 4-bit
adapter: qlora              # train a LoRA adapter
lora_r: 16                  # adapter size
datasets:
  - path: ./my_data.jsonl
    type: alpaca            # known instruction format
sequence_len: 2048          # max tokens per example
micro_batch_size: 2
num_epochs: 3
learning_rate: 0.0002
output_dir: ./out

Then you launch training with one line:

axolotl train config.yaml

The word epoch means one full pass over your whole dataset; num_epochs: 3 means the model sees every example three times. Hyperparameters are the dials you set before training that shape how it learns, as opposed to the weights the model learns by itself.

Where it fits in the ecosystem

Axolotl does not invent new training math. It orchestrates existing pieces. PEFT provides the LoRA adapters, the TRL Library provides trainers for methods like DPO (training a model to prefer better answers over worse ones), and Hugging Face provides the models and tokenizers (the code that splits text into tokens). Axolotl is the conductor, not the orchestra. Because the orchestra is the same one everyone else uses, your trained model is fully compatible with the wider toolset — there is no lock-in. It also supports multi-GPU training and advanced data formats out of the box, which is why teams reach for it once a job grows past one card.

A common real-world flow looks like this: you collect a few thousand examples of the behavior you want, format them, point a config at a small base model, run a cheap QLoRA pass overnight, and test the result the next morning. If it looks promising, you scale the same config to a bigger model or more GPUs by changing two or three lines. The config is the unit of work you iterate on.

AxolotlUnsloth
Config-file driven (YAML)Python-script driven
Many models, multi-GPU, many methodsOne GPU, heavily speed-optimized kernels
Great for reproducible, scaled jobsFastest single-GPU training and lowest memory
Wraps PEFT/TRL/HF broadlyCustom hand-tuned code paths

Both share the same goal — easy fine-tuning — but pick different routes. Unsloth chases raw speed on one card; Axolotl chases breadth and reproducibility. Many people prototype in one and scale in the other.

💡 The hardest part of using Axolotl is rarely the tool. It is your dataset and choosing the right type so your data is formatted into the chat or instruction shape the model expects. Get the data right and the YAML almost writes itself.
mentalModel: Axolotl is a bread machine for fine-tuning: you pour in your ingredients (a base model and a dataset), pick a program (LoRA, QLoRA, or full fine-tune) on a single recipe card (the YAML config), press start, and it does the kneading and baking — wrapping Hugging Face, PEFT, and TRL underneath.
mistakes: Picking the wrong dataset type. The type field tells Axolotl how to turn your raw rows into the prompt format the model was trained on. If you pick alpaca when your data is really chat-shaped, the model learns the wrong template and your results suffer. Match the type to your data and to the model's expected chat format.

Confusing Axolotl with a model. Axolotl is a training tool, not an LLM. It produces an adapter or a fine-tuned model; it does not answer prompts itself. To run the result you still need an inference tool like vLLM or Ollama.

Setting sequence_len too high for your VRAM. Longer sequences blow up memory fast because of the KV cache and activations. A run that fits at 2048 tokens can crash at 8192. Start small and raise it only if memory allows.

Forgetting that the output is just an adapter. With adapter: qlora, Axolotl saves a small LoRA file, not a full model. To get a standalone model you must merge the adapter into the base afterward — see Adapter Tuning.

Ignoring the sample-packing and chat-template options. Axolotl can pack many short examples into one sequence for speed, and can apply a model's exact chat template automatically. Skipping these defaults can mean slower training or a model that never learns the right turn-by-turn format.

Blaming the tool when training diverges. If loss explodes or the model outputs garbage, the cause is usually data or a too-high learning rate, not Axolotl. Lower learning_rate, inspect a few formatted examples, and validate your dataset before assuming a bug.
exercise:
Write a minimal Axolotl YAML config that fine-tunes a small open model (e.g. an 8B Llama or a 3B Qwen) with QLoRA on a tiny instruction dataset of about 100 examples in a .jsonl file. Set load_in_4bit: true, adapter: qlora, lora_r: 16, num_epochs: 1, and a small micro_batch_size. Run axolotl train config.yaml and watch the loss number drop over the run.

Stretch goal: After training, merge the adapter into the base model, export it to GGUF format, and load it in Ollama to chat with your fine-tuned model locally.
solution: Hint: Each row of your .jsonl should look like {"instruction": "...", "input": "", "output": "..."} if you use type: alpaca. A healthy run shows the training loss starting around 1–2 and drifting downward; a flat or exploding loss means the learning rate or data is off. With 100 examples and one epoch, do not expect magic — the point is to see the full pipeline work end to end. For the stretch goal, Axolotl has a built-in merge step (axolotl merge-lora config.yaml), and tools like llama.cpp handle the GGUF conversion that Ollama then loads.
summary: Axolotl turns fine-tuning into filling out one YAML config file and running one command. It orchestrates Hugging Face, PEFT, and TRL under the hood, supports LoRA, QLoRA, and full fine-tunes across one or many GPUs, and shines at reproducible, scaled jobs. The real work is preparing a clean dataset and matching its format; the tool handles the rest.

keyTerms.def: An open-source fine-tuning tool driven by a single YAML config file. It wraps Hugging Face, PEFT, and TRL so you train models without writing training code.
A plain text file of setting: value pairs that defines an Axolotl run — base model, dataset, method, and hyperparameters — making the recipe easy to share and reproduce.
The pretrained model you start fine-tuning from, such as Llama-3.1-8B. Axolotl adapts it to your data rather than training from scratch.
A small set of trainable weights (LoRA/QLoRA) that Axolotl learns instead of changing the whole model. You merge it into the base afterward to get a standalone model.
One full pass over your entire dataset during training. num_epochs: 3 means the model sees every example three times.
A dial you set before training that shapes how learning happens — like learning rate, batch size, or number of epochs — distinct from the weights the model learns itself.
The Axolotl field that tells it how to format your raw rows into the prompt shape the model expects (e.g. alpaca for instruction data, or a chat template).
Combining several short training examples into one longer sequence so the GPU wastes less effort on padding, speeding up training.
